chapters.title: Lesson 1
chapters.summary:
== Lesson 1: Basic Greeting ==
Perhaps this lesson should be expanded.

sections.heading: Lesson 1
sections.body:
== Lesson 1: Basic Greeting ==
Perhaps this lesson should be expanded.


=== Dialogue ===
Jane: ᎣᏏᏲ, ᏙᎯᏧ? o-si-yo, to-hi-tsu? (English: hello, how are you?)
John: ᎣᏍᏓ, ᏂᎯᎾ? o-s-da, ni-hi-na? (English: good, and you?)
Jane: ᎣᏍᏓᏛ o-s-da-dv (English: good)
John: ᎰᏩ! ho-wa! (English: alright!)
Jane: ᎦᏙ ᏕᏣᏙᎠ? Ga-do de-tsa-do-a? (English: What is your name?)
John: ᏣᏂ ᏓᏆᏙᎠ tsa-ni da-gwa-do-a (English: I am called John)
Jane: ᎭᏢ ᎯᏁᎳ? ha-tlv hi-ne-la? (English: Where do you live?)
John: ᏓᎵᏆ ᏥᏁᎳ. da-li-gwa tsi-ne-la (English: I live in Tahlequa)


=== Exercise ===
Unless you have printed this out, get a clean piece of paper. Without looking at the top, fill in the Cherokee words. (In syllabary as far as you remember, and in Latin)

Jane: (1)_______________ (hello, how are you?)
John: (2)_________________ (good, and you?)
Jane: (3)________ (fine) (in reply to the second person)
John: (4)_______ (alright!)
Answers: Here are the answers for questions 1-4:  (1) ᎣᏏᏲ, ᏙᎯᏧ?  o-si-yo, to-hi-tsu (2) ᎣᏍᏓ, ᏂᎯᎾ? o-s-da, ni-hi-na? (3) ᎣᏍᏓᏛ o-s-da-dv (4)ᎰᏩ! ho-wa! 


== Notes for those who correct the book ==
ᎣᏏᏧ (O-si-tsu) mean "I am fine"
ᏂᎾ (ni-na) or ᏂᎯᎾ (ni-hi-na) for And You?
ᏓᏆᏙᎠ da-gwa-to-a or ᏓᏆᏙ da-gwa-do

chapters.title: Lesson 3
chapters.summary:
=== Word Order ===
Word order is quite flexible in Cherokee.
Place the most salient (important/new) information at the beginning of the sentence.

sections.heading: Lesson 3
sections.body:
=== Word Order ===
Word order is quite flexible in Cherokee.
Place the most salient (important/new) information at the beginning of the sentence.


=== Negation ===
Use irrealis yi- prefix along with hla (not)


=== "When" ===
Use irrealis yi- prefix by itself.


=== Conjunctions ===


==== And ====
(1) ale, OR
(2) -hno suffix after second element


==== But ====

chapters.title: Lesson 9
chapters.summary:
=== Expressing Feelings and Emotions With Verbs Instead of Adjectives ===
Some expressions that would be phrased in English like "I am tired," for example, rather use a single verb in Cherokee meaning "to be [a certain feeling/emotion]" instead of a combination of a verb (like "t

sections.heading: Lesson 9
sections.body:
=== Expressing Feelings and Emotions With Verbs Instead of Adjectives ===
Some expressions that would be phrased in English like "I am tired," for example, rather use a single verb in Cherokee meaning "to be [a certain feeling/emotion]" instead of a combination of a verb (like "to be" in English) with an adjective. These feeling and emotion verbs should be simply memorized.


=== Vocabulary ===
tired
happy
sad
excited
angry
jealous, envious
disappointed

chapters.title: Introduction
chapters.summary:
Grammar of the Cherokee language
Cherokee or Tsalagi (ᏣᎳᎩ ᎦᏬᏂᎯᏍᏗ, Tsalagi Gawonihisdi [dʒalaˈɡî ɡawónihisˈdî]) is an endangered-to-moribund Iroquoian language and the native language of the Cherokee people.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
Grammar of the Cherokee language
Cherokee or Tsalagi (ᏣᎳᎩ ᎦᏬᏂᎯᏍᏗ, Tsalagi Gawonihisdi [dʒalaˈɡî ɡawónihisˈdî]) is an endangered-to-moribund Iroquoian language and the native language of the Cherokee people.

All presented prefixes and suffixes will be in the Latin script.

chapters.title: General
chapters.summary: Cherokee is a polysynthetic verb-heavy nominative–accusative language[1][2] with a non-productive incorporation system. Verbs may be inflected with a large number of suffixes and prefixes that express a range of properties, including subject and/or object agreement, tense and asp

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: Cherokee is a polysynthetic verb-heavy nominative–accusative language[1][2] with a non-productive incorporation system. Verbs may be inflected with a large number of suffixes and prefixes that express a range of properties, including subject and/or object agreement, tense and aspect, and evidentiality.

chapters.title: Verbs
chapters.summary: Verbs are the central, obligatory part of Cherokee sentences (with the exception of affirmative copular sentences in the present tense). Verbs in Cherokee fall into two conjugation classes, which determine the forms of pronominal prefixes in most tenses.[3]

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: Verbs are the central, obligatory part of Cherokee sentences (with the exception of affirmative copular sentences in the present tense). Verbs in Cherokee fall into two conjugation classes, which determine the forms of pronominal prefixes in most tenses.[3]

Pronoun prefixes (-2)
All Cherokee verbs occur with a pronoun prefix that expresses person properties of a subject argument, object argument, or some combinations of subject and object properties. There are two sets of pronoun prefixes, set A and set B (also known as set I and set II). Only set B prefixes are used with completive and infinitive stems. In other tenses, the choice of prefix is determined by the identity of the verb. These are only present if the verb is intransitive, for transitive verbs one joined set is used. Mark that the exclusive forms are generated by taking the corresponding 2nd person form and adding an "O".

Compound pronouns
Compound pronoun prefixes express a combination of subject and object properties. Some unique forms are used when both the subject and object of the verb are participants in the conversation (first and second person). Other compound pronouns are used with combinations of a first or second person subject and a third person object. These forms depend on the animacy of the third person object. A prominent feature of transitive pronouns is the -y- insertion. Often the pronoun relationship is both ways and can be used for either action, ambiguity is ensured by patterns of changes within the verb stem.

Verbstem (0)
Final and non-final suffixes (1)
The slots of both final and non-final suffixes cannot be properly dissected and thus the attribute final only means that one of these is obligated at all times. For some suffixes even the exact position is disputed amongst native speakers. In the following table final suffixes are marked bold. Because of the sheer amount of suffixes and the number of exceptions it is more efficient for students to learn the most common fused forms first (mostly those with -'i). Markers of aspect and tense are found in this category.

chapters.title: Nouns
chapters.summary: Nouns often occur as arguments of verbs. They can also be used predicatively, without a copular verb. Nouns are generally much less inflected than verbs. However, some nouns can occur with inflectional affixes similar to those found on verbs, mainly pronominal prefixes and the di

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: Nouns often occur as arguments of verbs. They can also be used predicatively, without a copular verb. Nouns are generally much less inflected than verbs. However, some nouns can occur with inflectional affixes similar to those found on verbs, mainly pronominal prefixes and the distributive prefix.

sections.heading: Prefixes
sections.body:
The number of prefixes nouns can take is comparably smaller than that of verbs, but since nouns can be used predicatively (nouns can be translated roughly as "to be X") they can occur with the pronominal prefixes that also occur on verbs, if not exchanged with one of the three below.

prefix

_C

_V

_u

meaning

di

di

j

d

plural

(inanimate)

a

human

n

ni

n

plural

(animate)

Thus plural words for humans take an(i)- as marker (even if the a was optional).
examples: The number of prefixes nouns can take is comparably smaller than that of verbs, but since nouns can be used predicatively (nouns can be translated roughly as "to be X") they can occur with the pronominal prefixes that also occur on verbs, i

sections.heading: Conjugation - to be someone
sections.body: For persons and their belongings normally set A prefixes are used. The second person forms also serve as vocative.

sections.heading: Possession - to belong to someone
sections.body: Every bodypart has to be possessed, unless it is understood as detached or as a general image for example. The bodyparts take both set A and set B prefixes.

sections.heading: Kinship - to be someone in relation to someone
sections.body: To explain kinship (or other relationships) the transitive compound pronouns are used. In this case the agent part expresses who it is (ex. I am a father) whilst the formerly patient part indicates to whom the relationship takes place (I-him-father = I, his father).

sections.heading: Suffixes
sections.body:
The same with the prefixes; the presented suffixes are exclusive for nouns, whilst these can also take numerous verb suffixes.

chapters.title: Adjectives
chapters.summary: Adjectives can also be treated as verbs of certain qualities, they precede the noun they qualify.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: Adjectives can also be treated as verbs of certain qualities, they precede the noun they qualify.

chapters.title: Adverbs
chapters.summary: Adverbs are essentially adjectives preceding the qualified verb.

sections.heading: Adverbs
sections.body: Adverbs are essentially adjectives preceding the qualified verb.
